1:
"Therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection; not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works, and of faith toward God," The witnesses note the call to leave the first principles and go on to maturity. On "leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection," the grammatical voices note the charge to advance. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation following the rebuke of their dullness (5:11-14): "leaving (aphentes — leaving behind, not abandoning but moving on from) the principles (the elementary teaching, the ABCs) of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on (be borne on) unto perfection (teleioteta — maturity, full growth)" — believers are to advance from the rudiments to mature understanding.

On "not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works, and of faith toward God," the witnesses note the foundation. Barnes and Calvin note the first two of six foundational topics: "repentance from dead works (turning from works that bring death, sinful or lifeless deeds) and faith toward God" — the elementary first steps, not to be laid again repeatedly but built upon.

The puritan Henry notes the aim: not to despise the foundation, but to build on it; the believer is not to remain forever at the first principles but to press on to maturity in the knowledge of Christ. The verse displays the call to leave the first principles and go on to maturity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "leaving (moving on from) the principles (elementary teaching) of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection (maturity)," advancing from rudiments to mature understanding (Barnes, Robertson); "the foundation of repentance from dead works and faith toward God," the first two foundational topics not to be laid again (Barnes, Calvin); building on the foundation, pressing to maturity (Henry). The verse displays the call to advance. The verse marks the leaving: the first principles. The verse displays the goal: perfection, maturity. The verse displays the foundation: repentance and faith. The verse displays the aim: build, not relay. The call displays believers urged to mature. The verse displays the principles: left. The verse displays the maturity: pursued. The verse displays the foundation: laid once. The call to leave the first principles and go on to maturity displays the exhortation that opens the chapter: believers are not to linger forever at the elementary teaching — repentance and faith — but to move on from the foundation, building upon it toward full Christian maturity. The verse displays the call to advance, completing the list of foundational doctrines (v.2).
2:
"Of the doctrine of baptisms, and of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead, and of eternal judgment." The brief verse notes the remaining foundational doctrines. The grammatical voices note the list completed. Barnes and Robertson note the four further foundational topics (with repentance and faith, v.1, making six): "the doctrine of baptisms (washings, instruction about the various ceremonial washings and Christian baptism), and of laying on of hands (the rite by which gifts or blessing or office were conferred)" — the elementary teachings about the ordinances.

On "resurrection of the dead, and eternal judgment," the witnesses note the eschatological foundations. Barnes and Calvin note the last two: "the resurrection of the dead (the raising of the body) and eternal judgment (the final, everlasting judgment)" — the basic truths about the last things; foundational doctrines every catechumen learned.

The grammatical-historical note the catechetical character: these six items (repentance, faith, baptisms, laying on of hands, resurrection, judgment) form the basic catechism — the rudiments of Christian instruction from which the readers are to advance to maturity. The verse displays the remaining foundational doctrines.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the four further topics — "baptisms (washings, including Christian baptism) and laying on of hands (the rite conferring gifts or office)," the teaching about ordinances (Barnes, Robertson); "resurrection of the dead and eternal judgment," the basic truths of the last things (Barnes, Calvin); the six forming the basic catechism, the rudiments (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the foundation. The verse marks the ordinances: baptisms, laying on of hands. The verse displays the last things: resurrection, judgment. The verse displays the list: six items. The verse displays the character: catechism. The doctrines display the rudiments of Christian instruction. The verse displays the baptisms: taught. The verse displays the resurrection: foundational. The verse displays the judgment: eternal. The remaining foundational doctrines display the basic catechism the readers are to move beyond: the teaching about baptisms and laying on of hands, and the resurrection of the dead and eternal judgment — the elementary rudiments from which believers must advance to maturity. The verse displays the foundational teaching, which the writer will build upon if God permit (v.3).
3:
"And this will we do, if God permit." The brief verse notes the resolve to advance, if God permit. The grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the writer's resolve: "this will we do (we will go on to maturity, advance to the higher teaching), if God permit (if God allows, grants it)" — the writer purposes to lead them on to the deeper truths, conditioned on God's permission.

On "this will we do," the witnesses note the intention. Barnes and Calvin note that the writer intends to proceed to the higher doctrine (the priesthood of Christ after Melchizedek), not lingering at the foundation; he will lead them forward.

On "if God permit," the grammatical-historical note the dependence: the advance is "if God permit" — recognizing that progress in spiritual understanding is God's gift; even the teacher's leading and the hearers' growth depend on God's enabling grace; and perhaps a hint that, if they are hardened (vv.4-6), the advance may not be possible. The verse displays the resolve to advance, if God permit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the writer's resolve — "this will we do (advance to maturity), if God permit," purposing to lead them to the deeper truths (Barnes, Robertson); intending to proceed to the higher doctrine, not lingering at the foundation (Barnes, Calvin); "if God permit," progress being God's gift, the advance depending on his enabling grace (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resolve. The verse marks the purpose: this will we do. The verse displays the advance: to maturity. The verse displays the condition: if God permit. The verse displays the dependence: on God. The resolve displays the writer leading them on under God. The verse displays the doing: resolved. The verse displays the permission: God's. The verse displays the advance: conditioned. The resolve to advance, if God permit, displays the writer's intention and his dependence: he purposes to lead the readers on from the foundation to the deeper truths — if God permits, for spiritual progress is God's gift, and the teacher leans on his enabling grace. The verse displays the conditioned resolve, sharpened by the solemn warning against apostasy (vv.4-6).
4:
"For it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost," The witnesses note the description of those who experienced the great blessings — opening the apostasy crux. On the persons described, interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with Wesley, Clarke) holds the description — "once enlightened (illumined by the gospel), have tasted of the heavenly gift (experienced salvation, forgiveness), were made partakers of the Holy Ghost (shared the Spirit's renewing influences)" — to portray true Christians; Barnes argues these phrases cannot properly apply to any but the regenerate, and the warning is to keep true believers from apostasy. The Reformed Calvin and Gill hold that the reprobate may receive "some taste" of grace — a temporary illumination, a "temporal faith" (Mark 4:17) that vanishes — without being truly regenerate; the elect cannot finally fall.

On "once enlightened," the witnesses note the word. Barnes and Robertson note "once (hapax — once for all) enlightened (photisthentas)" — the early church used "enlightenment" of baptism and conversion; the illumining of the mind by the gospel.

On the experiences, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley note their reality: tasting the heavenly gift (remission of sins) and partaking of the Holy Ghost (the witness and fruit of the Spirit) are real spiritual experiences, marks of genuine grace. The verse displays the description of those who experienced the great blessings.

The witnesses agree the substance: the description piles up the marks of Christian experience — enlightenment, tasting the heavenly gift, partaking of the Holy Ghost — the richest language of grace (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Wesley). But whether these are true Christians divides them: Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley hold the persons are genuine believers (the language cannot apply to the unregenerate); Calvin and Gill hold the reprobate may have a temporary taste of grace, a "temporal faith" that is not true regeneration, the elect never finally falling. The verse displays the persons crux (resolved at v.6). The verse marks the enlightenment: once for all. The verse displays the taste: the heavenly gift. The verse displays the partaking: the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the debate: true believers or temporary professors. The description displays souls who shared the great blessings. The verse displays the enlightening: of the mind. The verse displays the tasting: of grace. The verse displays the Spirit: partaken. The description of those who experienced the great blessings displays the opening of the apostasy crux: those once enlightened, who tasted the heavenly gift and partook of the Holy Ghost — read as true Christians (Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) or as professors with a temporary taste of grace short of regeneration (Calvin, Gill). The verse displays the experienced, who tasted yet more (v.5).
5:
"And have tasted the good word of God, and the powers of the world to come," The brief verse notes the further blessings tasted: the good word and the powers of the age to come. The grammatical voices note the experiences. Barnes and Robertson note the continuation of the description: "have tasted the good word of God (experienced the excellence and sweetness of God's word, the truth of the gospel) and the powers of the world to come (dynameis mellontos aionos — the energies of the coming age)" — they had relished the gospel's truth and felt the powers of the Messianic age.

On "the good word of God," the witnesses note its sweetness. Barnes and Clarke note that to taste "the good word of God" is to experience the excellence of the gospel, to find delight and relish in its truth — a mark of the renewed heart, which loves the word.

On "the powers of the world to come," interpreters note its sense. Barnes and Clarke note two views: the "powers of the world to come" may mean the miraculous powers and gifts that attended the gospel age (Clarke leans here — the miracles confirming the gospel), or the foretastes of heavenly blessedness, the energies of the coming age experienced now (Wesley, Barnes lean here — the hope full of immortality). The verse displays the further blessings tasted.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the continuation — "tasted the good word of God (experienced the gospel's excellence and sweetness) and the powers of the world to come (the energies of the coming age)," relishing the truth and feeling the powers of the Messianic age (Barnes, Robertson); "the good word" the gospel's truth, delighted in by the renewed heart (Barnes, Clarke); "the powers of the world to come" either the confirming miracles (Clarke) or the foretastes of heavenly blessedness (Wesley, Barnes). The verse displays the further experiences. The verse marks the word: tasted as good. The verse displays the powers: of the coming age. The verse displays the sweetness: relished. The verse displays the foretaste: of heaven. The blessings display the rich spiritual experiences shared. The verse displays the word: good. The verse displays the powers: felt. The verse displays the age: to come. The further blessings tasted — the good word and the powers of the age to come — display the completion of the description: those warned had relished the excellence of God's word and felt the powers of the Messianic age, whether its confirming miracles or its foretastes of heavenly glory. The verse displays the full description, against the dread possibility of falling away (v.6).
6:
"If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance; seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh, and put him to an open shame." The witnesses note the impossibility of renewing the apostate — the great perseverance crux. On "if they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance," interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes holds the persons are true Christians, but the falling-away is hypothetical: were a true Christian to apostatize, it would be impossible to renew him — yet in fact none ever do (John 10:28; 1 John 2:19); the warning is the very means God uses to preserve them. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley take it as a real possibility: true believers can wholly and wilfully apostatize and be irrecoverable; Wesley: "not a supposition, but a plain relation of fact" of "wilful total apostates"; Clarke rejects the inserted "if" as Calvinist bias (the Greek is "having fallen away"). The Reformed Calvin and Gill hold the apostates were never truly regenerate — only reprobates with a temporary taste of grace; the truly elect cannot finally fall.

On "impossible... to renew," interpreters note the impossibility. Barnes (with Wesley) takes "impossible (adynaton)" as absolute — not merely difficult; should one apostatize, his case is hopeless. Ellicott (and Calvin's alternative) take the impossibility to relate to human action — impossible for the teacher to renew such by ordinary means.

On "seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh," the witnesses note the reason. Barnes and Calvin note that the apostate, by his wilful rejection, "crucifies to himself the Son of God afresh (treats Christ as the Jews did, judging him worthy of the cross) and puts him to an open shame" — re-enacting in spirit the crucifixion, holding Christ up to public contempt; this aggravated sin makes renewal impossible. The verse displays the impossibility of renewing the apostate.

The witnesses agree the gravity: total, wilful apostasy — a renouncing of Christ that "crucifies him afresh" and exposes him to shame — leaves the apostate in a hopeless state; renewal to repentance is impossible (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). But the deep questions divide them. Whether true believers are in view: Barnes (yes, but hypothetically — God preserves them); Wesley and Clarke (yes, and they really may fall, irrecoverably — Arminian); Calvin and Gill (no — only reprobates with a temporary taste, the elect never falling). Whether "impossible" is absolute (Barnes, Wesley) or relates to human renewal (Ellicott, Calvin's alt). The verse displays the perseverance crux. The verse marks the falling: away. The verse displays the impossibility: to renew. The verse displays the debate: true believers fall, hypothetical, or only professors. The verse displays the reason: crucify Christ afresh. The crux displays the dread of apostasy and the security of the saints. The verse displays the apostasy: total. The verse displays the renewal: impossible. The verse displays the shame: put on Christ. The impossibility of renewing the apostate displays the great perseverance crux: those who fall wholly away, crucifying the Son of God afresh, cannot be renewed to repentance — read as a hypothetical warning to preserved believers (Barnes), as the real and irrecoverable fall of true Christians (Wesley, Clarke), or as the end of reprobates who never truly believed (Calvin, Gill). The verse displays the warning against apostasy, illustrated by the fruitful and barren ground (vv.7-8).
7:
"For the earth which drinketh in the rain that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs meet for them by whom it is dressed, receiveth blessing from God:" The brief verse notes the fruitful ground that receives blessing. The grammatical voices note the parable of the soil. Barnes and Robertson note the illustration of the warning: "the earth which drinketh in (absorbs) the rain that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs (useful vegetation) meet (fit, profitable) for them by whom it is dressed (cultivated)" — ground that, watered by frequent rain, yields a useful crop for its cultivators.

On "receiveth blessing from God," the witnesses note the reward. Barnes and Chrysostom note that such fruitful ground "receiveth blessing from God" — it is blessed, fruitful, approved; the figure of the believer who, receiving the means of grace (the rain of gospel teaching), brings forth the fruit of holiness and is blessed.

The grammatical-historical note the application: the "rain" is the repeated teaching and grace the readers had received (vv.4-5); the fruitful ground pictures those who use it well and are blessed — over against the barren ground (v.8) that pictures the apostate. The verse displays the fruitful ground that receives blessing.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the parable — "the earth which drinketh in (absorbs) the rain that cometh oft, and bringeth forth herbs (useful crop) meet for them by whom it is dressed (cultivated)," ground watered by rain yielding a useful crop (Barnes, Robertson); "receiveth blessing from God," the fruitful ground blessed and approved (Barnes, Chrysostom); the rain the gospel grace received, the fruitful ground the believer who uses it well (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruitful ground. The verse marks the rain: drunk in. The verse displays the fruit: useful herbs. The verse displays the blessing: from God. The verse displays the application: the fruitful believer. The parable displays the well-watered soil bearing fruit and blessed. The verse displays the earth: drinking. The verse displays the herbs: fit. The verse displays the blessing: received. The fruitful ground that receives blessing displays the bright side of the parable that illustrates the warning: as ground watered by frequent rain bears a useful crop and is blessed by God, so those who receive the gospel's grace and bring forth the fruit of holiness are blessed. The verse displays the fruitful soil, over against the barren ground that is cursed (v.8).
8:
"But that which beareth thorns and briers is rejected, and is nigh unto cursing; whose end is to be burned." The brief verse notes the barren ground that is rejected and burned. The grammatical voices note the dark side of the parable. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "that which beareth thorns and briers (worthless, harmful growth, despite the same rain) is rejected (disapproved, adokimos — reprobate), and is nigh unto cursing (close to being cursed)" — ground that, though watered, yields only thorns is worthless and near to the curse.

On "rejected, and nigh unto cursing," the witnesses note the doom. Barnes and Calvin note that such ground is "rejected" (cast aside as useless) and "nigh unto cursing" (on the verge of the curse) — the figure of the apostate who, having received the same grace, brings forth no fruit but evil and is near to condemnation.

On "whose end is to be burned," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the final doom: "whose end is to be burned (the burning of the worthless field, the figure of destruction)" — the barren ground is finally burned; the apostate's end is destruction. The verse displays the barren ground that is rejected and burned.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "that which beareth thorns and briers (worthless growth despite the rain) is rejected (reprobate), and nigh unto cursing," ground yielding only thorns worthless and near the curse (Barnes, Robertson); "rejected and nigh unto cursing," cast aside and on the verge of the curse, the figure of the fruitless apostate (Barnes, Calvin); "whose end is to be burned," finally destroyed, the apostate's end (Wesley). The verse displays the barren ground. The verse marks the growth: thorns and briers. The verse displays the verdict: rejected. The verse displays the nearness: to cursing. The verse displays the end: burned. The parable displays the fruitless soil cursed and burned. The verse displays the thorns: borne. The verse displays the rejection: deserved. The verse displays the burning: the end. The barren ground that is rejected and burned displays the dark side of the parable: ground that, though watered by the same rain, yields only thorns is worthless, near to the curse, and finally burned — the figure of those who receive the gospel's grace yet bring forth no fruit but apostasy, whose end is destruction. The verse displays the doom of the fruitless, over against the writer's better hope for the readers (v.9).
9:
"But, beloved, we are persuaded better things of you, and things that accompany salvation, though we thus speak." The witnesses note the writer's better hope for the readers. On "beloved, we are persuaded better things of you," the grammatical voices note the tender turn. Barnes and Robertson note the change from warning to encouragement: "beloved (a term of affection, used only here in the epistle), we are persuaded (confident, convinced) better things of you" — after the severe warning, the writer tenderly assures them he expects better of them than apostasy.

On "things that accompany salvation," the witnesses note the hope. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning: "things that accompany (belong to, are connected with) salvation" — he is persuaded that they possess the marks and fruits of genuine salvation, not the barren thorns of v.8; the better things are the evidences of true grace.

On "though we thus speak," the puritan Henry notes the pastoral wisdom: "though we thus speak" (though we have warned so severely) — the warning was not because he despaired of them, but to guard them; severe warning and warm confidence go together; he warns the loved, hoping the best of them. The verse displays the writer's better hope for the readers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tender turn — "beloved, we are persuaded (confident) better things of you," assuring them after the warning that he expects better than apostasy (Barnes, Robertson); "things that accompany salvation," the marks and fruits of genuine salvation, not the barren thorns (Barnes, Calvin); "though we thus speak," the severe warning given not from despair but to guard them, warning and confidence together (Henry). The verse displays the better hope. The verse marks the affection: beloved. The verse displays the confidence: better things. The verse displays the hope: things of salvation. The verse displays the wisdom: warning the loved. The hope displays the writer expecting the best of them. The verse displays the persuasion: confident. The verse displays the salvation: accompanied. The verse displays the speaking: severe yet loving. The writer's better hope for the readers displays the tenderness that follows the warning: though he has spoken so severely, he is persuaded of better things of them — the marks and fruits of genuine salvation — warning them not from despair but from love. The verse displays the confident hope, grounded in God's faithful remembrance (v.10).
10:
"For God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love, which ye have shewed toward his name, in that ye have ministered to the saints, and do minister." The witnesses note God's faithful remembrance of their work and love. On "God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love," the grammatical voices note the ground of confidence. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for his better hope: "God is not unrighteous (it would be contrary to his justice and faithfulness) to forget your work and labour of love" — God will not overlook or forget their faithful service; his justice guarantees that genuine works of love are remembered.

On "which ye have shewed toward his name," the witnesses note the motive. Barnes and Calvin note that their labour was "shewed toward his name (for God's sake, out of love to him)" — their service sprang from love to God; works done for his name's sake he will surely remember.

On "ye have ministered to the saints, and do minister," the puritan Henry notes the evidence: their love was shown "in that ye have ministered (served, helped) to the saints, and do minister" — past and continuing service to fellow-believers; their ongoing love to the saints was evidence of genuine grace, a fruit accompanying salvation (v.9). The verse displays God's faithful remembrance of their work and love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of confidence — "God is not unrighteous (contrary to his justice) to forget your work and labour of love," God not forgetting faithful service, his justice guaranteeing it (Barnes, Robertson); "shewed toward his name (for God's sake)," service springing from love to God (Barnes, Calvin); "ye have ministered to the saints, and do minister," past and continuing service to believers, evidence of grace (Henry). The verse displays God's remembrance. The verse marks the justice: not unrighteous. The verse displays the works: love and labour. The verse displays the motive: toward his name. The verse displays the evidence: ministering to saints. The remembrance displays God faithfully recalling their love. The verse displays the forgetting: refused. The verse displays the labour: of love. The verse displays the ministry: to saints. God's faithful remembrance of their work and love displays the ground of the writer's confidence: God is too just to forget their work and labour of love shown toward his name — their past and continuing ministry to the saints — these being the very fruits that accompany salvation. The verse displays their evidenced grace, urging diligence to the end (v.11).
11:
"And we desire that every one of you do shew the same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end:" The brief verse notes the desire that each show diligence to full assurance to the end. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note the writer's earnest wish: "we desire (earnestly wish) that every one of you (each individually) do shew the same diligence (spoude — earnestness, zeal)" — the same diligence they showed in love (v.10) is to be shown in pressing on; the exhortation is personal, to each.

On "to the full assurance of hope," the witnesses note the goal. Barnes and Calvin note the aim: "to the full assurance (plerophoria — full conviction, certainty) of hope" — that their diligence may issue in a firm, settled assurance of their hope of salvation; the goal is not doubt but full confidence.

On "unto the end," the puritan Henry notes the perseverance: this diligence and assurance are to continue "unto the end" — to the close of life; perseverance to the end is the mark; the believer is to press on diligently, not slacken, until the goal is reached. The verse displays the desire that each show diligence to full assurance to the end.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the earnest wish — "we desire that every one of you (each) do shew the same diligence (earnestness)," the same diligence shown in love now shown in pressing on, personally to each (Barnes, Robertson); "to the full assurance (full conviction) of hope," diligence issuing in settled assurance of salvation (Barnes, Calvin); "unto the end," continuing to the close of life, perseverance the mark (Henry). The verse displays the diligence urged. The verse marks the desire: every one. The verse displays the diligence: the same. The verse displays the goal: full assurance of hope. The verse displays the duration: unto the end. The exhortation displays each believer pressing on to assured hope. The verse displays the diligence: shown. The verse displays the assurance: full. The verse displays the end: persevered to. The desire that each show diligence to full assurance to the end displays the writer's pastoral aim: that every one of them press on with the same earnestness they showed in love — that their hope may grow into full assurance, persevering steadfastly to the very end. The verse displays the call to diligent perseverance, imitating the faithful (v.12).
12:
"That ye be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises." The brief verse notes the call to imitate those who through faith and patience inherit the promises. The grammatical voices note the negative and positive. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation: "that ye be not slothful (nothroi — sluggish, dull, the same word as 5:11), but followers (mimetai — imitators) of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises" — not sluggish but imitating the faithful who obtained the promises.

On "through faith and patience," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note the twofold means: "through faith (believing trust) and patience (makrothymia — long-suffering, patient endurance)" — the promises are inherited by faith that lays hold and patience that waits; faith and steadfast endurance together.

On "inherit the promises," the puritan Henry notes the examples: those who "inherit (obtain, come into possession of) the promises" — the saints of old (Abraham chief among them, vv.13-15) who, by faith and patient endurance, obtained what God promised; the readers are to follow their example. The verse displays the call to imitate those who inherit the promises.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "be not slothful (sluggish, dull), but followers (imitators) of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises," not sluggish but imitating the faithful (Barnes, Robertson); "through faith and patience (patient endurance)," the promises inherited by faith and steadfast endurance (Barnes, Calvin); "inherit the promises," the saints of old who obtained God's promises, to be imitated (Henry). The verse displays the call to imitate. The verse marks the negative: not slothful. The verse displays the positive: followers of the faithful. The verse displays the means: faith and patience. The verse displays the reward: inherit the promises. The call displays believers imitating the faithful who endured. The verse displays the sloth: avoided. The verse displays the imitation: of the faithful. The verse displays the promises: inherited. The call to imitate those who through faith and patience inherit the promises displays the pattern the writer sets: the readers are not to be sluggish but to follow the example of the faithful — who, by believing trust and patient endurance, came into possession of God's promises. The verse displays the example of the faithful, supremely Abraham (vv.13-15).
13:
"For when God made promise to Abraham, because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself," The brief verse notes God swearing by himself in his promise to Abraham. The grammatical voices note the divine oath. Barnes and Robertson note the example of Abraham, who obtained the promise: "when God made promise to Abraham (the promise of blessing and posterity, Genesis 22:16-17), because he could swear by no greater (there being none greater than himself to swear by), he sware by himself" — God confirmed his promise with an oath, sworn by his own being.

On "because he could swear by no greater," the witnesses note the uniqueness. Barnes and Calvin note that men swear by one greater (by God), but God, having none greater, swears by himself — by his own existence and faithfulness; the oath is grounded in God himself.

On "he sware by himself," the eastern Chrysostom notes the condescension: God's swearing by himself was a wonderful condescension to human weakness — to give the heirs of promise the fullest possible assurance, God added his oath to his bare word; he stooped to confirm his promise as men do, by oath. The verse displays God swearing by himself in his promise to Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine oath — "when God made promise to Abraham (Genesis 22:16-17), because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself," confirming his promise with an oath sworn by his own being (Barnes, Robertson); men swearing by the greater (God), but God having none greater swears by himself, grounded in his own faithfulness (Barnes, Calvin); God's condescension in adding his oath to give the fullest assurance (Chrysostom). The verse displays the oath to Abraham. The verse marks the promise: to Abraham. The verse displays the oath: sware by himself. The verse displays the reason: no greater. The verse displays the condescension: to assure. The oath displays God confirming his word by himself. The verse displays the promise: made. The verse displays the swearing: by himself. The verse displays the greater: none. God swearing by himself in his promise to Abraham displays the strength of God's confirmation: when he promised Abraham blessing and posterity, having none greater to swear by, he swore by himself — adding his oath to his word, that the heirs might have the fullest assurance. The verse displays the divine oath, whose promise Abraham obtained (vv.14-15).
14:
"Saying, Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee." The brief verse notes the sworn promise of blessing and multiplying. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the words of the oath (Genesis 22:17): "Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee" — the Hebrew idiom of the doubled verb (blessing... bless, multiplying... multiply) expressing the certainty and abundance of the promise; God will surely and richly bless and multiply Abraham.

On "blessing I will bless thee," the witnesses note the emphasis. Barnes and Calvin note that the repeated verb is a Hebraism for emphatic certainty: God will assuredly bless him — the blessing both certain and abundant; the promise of every good to Abraham and his seed.

On "multiplying I will multiply thee," the grammatical-historical note the posterity: "multiplying I will multiply thee" — the promise of a numerous seed, descendants as the stars and sand; the great covenant promise to Abraham, fulfilled in his offspring and supremely in Christ. The verse displays the sworn promise of blessing and multiplying.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Genesis 22:17) — "Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee," the doubled Hebrew verb expressing certainty and abundance (Barnes, Robertson); the repeated verb a Hebraism for emphatic certainty, the blessing certain and abundant (Barnes, Calvin); "multiplying I will multiply thee," the promise of a numerous seed, the covenant promise (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sworn promise. The verse marks the blessing: surely. The verse displays the multiplying: abundantly. The verse displays the idiom: doubled verb. The verse displays the content: blessing and seed. The promise displays God's certain blessing of Abraham. The verse displays the blessing: assured. The verse displays the multiplying: rich. The verse displays the certainty: emphatic. The sworn promise of blessing and multiplying displays the substance of God's oath to Abraham: he would surely and abundantly bless him and multiply his seed — the emphatic doubled verbs marking the certainty and richness of the covenant promise. The verse displays the certain promise, which Abraham patiently obtained (v.15).
15:
"And so, after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise." The brief verse notes Abraham obtaining the promise after patient endurance. The grammatical voices note the reward of patience. Barnes and Robertson note the outcome: "after he had patiently endured (waited with patience, long-suffering), he obtained the promise" — Abraham, by patient waiting, came at last into possession of what God promised; faith and patience (v.12) exemplified.

On "patiently endured," the witnesses note the long waiting. Barnes and Calvin note that Abraham waited long — for the birth of Isaac, and through many trials — patiently trusting God's promise; his patience under delay is the pattern for the readers.

On "obtained the promise," the grammatical-historical note the fulfillment: he "obtained the promise" — received the thing promised (the son, and the beginning of the seed), a pledge of the full inheritance; God's faithfulness was proved in Abraham's experience, encouraging the readers to like patience. The verse displays Abraham obtaining the promise after patient endurance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the outcome — "after he had patiently endured (waited with patience), he obtained the promise," Abraham by patient waiting coming into possession of God's promise (Barnes, Robertson); his long waiting (for Isaac, through trials) patiently trusting, the pattern for the readers (Barnes, Calvin); "obtained the promise," receiving the thing promised, a pledge of the full inheritance, God's faithfulness proved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reward of patience. The verse marks the endurance: patient. The verse displays the obtaining: the promise. The verse displays the waiting: long. The verse displays the proof: God's faithfulness. The outcome displays Abraham rewarded for patient faith. The verse displays the enduring: patient. The verse displays the promise: obtained. The verse displays the example: for them. Abraham obtaining the promise after patient endurance displays the example the writer holds up: Abraham, by patiently waiting and trusting through long delay and trial, came at last into possession of what God promised — faith and patience rewarded, encouraging the readers to like steadfastness. The verse displays the reward of patient faith, grounded in the certainty of God's oath (vv.16-18).
16:
"For men verily swear by the greater: and an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife." The brief verse notes the human oath that ends all strife. The grammatical voices note the principle of oaths. Barnes and Robertson note the general truth about human oaths: "men verily swear by the greater (by one greater than themselves, ultimately by God)" — in human affairs, men confirm their word by appealing to a greater, calling God to witness.

On "an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife," the witnesses note the oath's function. Barnes and Calvin note the purpose: "an oath for confirmation (to confirm a statement) is to them an end (terminus, settlement) of all strife (dispute, contradiction)" — the oath settles disputes finally; once a matter is confirmed by oath, the controversy is ended; the oath is the final guarantee among men.

The grammatical-historical note the application: this human custom illustrates God's oath (vv.13-14) — as an oath ends all dispute among men, so God's oath gives the heirs of promise the most absolute assurance; the argument runs from the lesser (human oaths) to the greater (God's). The verse displays the human oath that ends all strife.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "men verily swear by the greater (one greater than themselves, God)," confirming their word by appeal to a greater (Barnes, Robertson); "an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife," the oath settling disputes finally, ending controversy (Barnes, Calvin); this human custom illustrating God's oath, arguing from lesser to greater (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the oath's function. The verse marks the swearing: by the greater. The verse displays the confirmation: by oath. The verse displays the function: ends all strife. The verse displays the application: to God's oath. The principle displays the oath as the final human guarantee. The verse displays the men: swearing. The verse displays the oath: confirming. The verse displays the strife: ended. The human oath that ends all strife displays the principle that illumines God's oath: as men swear by one greater and an oath settles every dispute finally, so God's oath gives his promise the most absolute confirmation — the argument moving from the human custom to the divine assurance. The verse displays the confirming oath, applied to God's immutable counsel (v.17).
17:
"Wherein God, willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath:" The witnesses note God confirming his immutable counsel to the heirs by an oath. On "God, willing more abundantly to shew... the immutability of his counsel," the grammatical voices note the divine purpose. Barnes and Robertson note God's gracious intention: "God, willing more abundantly (more fully, beyond his bare word) to shew (demonstrate) unto the heirs of promise (those who inherit the promise, believers) the immutability (unchangeableness) of his counsel (his purpose, plan)" — God wished to make abundantly clear that his purpose cannot change.

On "the heirs of promise," the witnesses note the recipients. Barnes and Calvin note that "the heirs of promise" are not Abraham only but all believers, the spiritual seed who inherit the promise; God confirmed his counsel for their sake, that they might be fully assured.

On "confirmed it by an oath," the grammatical-historical note the means: God "confirmed it (interposed, guaranteed it) by an oath" — to his unchangeable word he added his oath, a double security; not that God's word needs confirming, but that he condescends to give the heirs the strongest possible ground of assurance. The verse displays God confirming his immutable counsel by an oath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine purpose — "God, willing more abundantly (more fully than by his word alone) to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability (unchangeableness) of his counsel (purpose)," wishing to make abundantly clear that his purpose cannot change (Barnes, Robertson); "the heirs of promise" all believers, the spiritual seed, for whose assurance God confirmed his counsel (Barnes, Calvin); "confirmed it by an oath," adding his oath to his word, a double security, condescending to assure them (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confirmed counsel. The verse marks the purpose: shew the immutability. The verse displays the counsel: unchangeable. The verse displays the heirs: of promise. The verse displays the means: an oath. The confirmation displays God assuring the heirs by oath. The verse displays the willing: God's. The verse displays the immutability: shown. The verse displays the oath: added. God confirming his immutable counsel to the heirs by an oath displays the abundance of God's gracious assurance: wishing to show the heirs of promise beyond all doubt that his purpose cannot change, God added his oath to his word — a double security for their full confidence. The verse displays the oath-confirmed counsel, the ground of strong consolation (v.18).
18:
"That by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie, we might have a strong consolation, who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us:" The witnesses note the strong consolation grounded in God's word and oath. On "by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie," the grammatical voices note the double ground. Barnes and Robertson note the two unchangeable things: "two immutable things (the promise and the oath, God's word and his sworn confirmation), in which it was impossible for God to lie" — two grounds, each absolutely sure, for God cannot lie; the believer's assurance rests on a double, unbreakable foundation.

On "we might have a strong consolation," the witnesses note the comfort. Barnes and Calvin note the design: "that... we might have a strong (mighty, powerful) consolation (encouragement, comfort)" — the purpose of the double assurance is the believer's strong comfort; the unchangeableness of God's word and oath gives powerful encouragement to the soul.

On "who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us," the eastern Cyril notes the refuge: this comfort is for those "who have fled for refuge (as to a sanctuary, the figure of the fugitive fleeing to the city of refuge or the altar) to lay hold upon (seize, grasp) the hope set before us" — believers, fleeing from danger, lay hold of the hope of the gospel as their refuge; the imagery of the fleeing soul grasping the hope. The verse displays the strong consolation grounded in God's word and oath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the double ground — "two immutable things (the promise and the oath), in which it was impossible for God to lie," two absolutely sure grounds, God being unable to lie (Barnes, Robertson); "that we might have a strong consolation (mighty comfort)," the double assurance designed for the believer's strong encouragement (Barnes, Calvin); "who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us," believers fleeing to grasp the gospel hope as their refuge (Cyril). The verse displays the strong consolation. The verse marks the ground: two immutable things. The verse displays the impossibility: God cannot lie. The verse displays the comfort: strong consolation. The verse displays the refuge: laying hold of hope. The consolation displays the fleeing soul anchored in God's word and oath. The verse displays the immutable: two things. The verse displays the lie: impossible. The verse displays the hope: laid hold. The strong consolation grounded in God's word and oath displays the believer's powerful comfort: by two unchangeable things — God's promise and his oath, in which it is impossible for him to lie — those who have fled for refuge to grasp the gospel hope have strong consolation, an unbreakable ground of assurance. The verse displays the hope laid hold of, which is the anchor of the soul (v.19).
19:
"Which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast, and which entereth into that within the veil;" The witnesses note the hope as an anchor entering within the veil. On "which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the beautiful image: "which hope we have as an anchor (ankyran) of the soul, both sure (asphale — firm, not slipping) and stedfast (bebaian — steady, immovable)" — the believer's hope is the anchor of the soul, holding it firm and steady amid the storms of life; sure in itself, steadfast in its hold.

On "anchor of the soul," the witnesses note the security. Barnes and Calvin note the aptness of the figure: as an anchor holds a ship secure against wind and wave, so hope holds the soul firm against the trials and temptations that would drive it from its mooring; the soul is anchored, not adrift.

On "which entereth into that within the veil," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the anchorage: the anchor of hope "entereth into that within the veil (the inner sanctuary, the Holy of Holies, here heaven itself)" — unlike an earthly anchor that grips the seabed below, this anchor reaches up and within, into heaven, fastened to Christ behind the veil; the soul's hope is anchored not in the shifting world but in the unseen, eternal sanctuary where Christ is. The verse displays the hope as an anchor entering within the veil.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure (firm) and stedfast (immovable)," hope the anchor holding the soul firm amid storms (Barnes, Robertson); as an anchor holds a ship, so hope holds the soul against trials, anchored not adrift (Barnes, Calvin); "which entereth into that within the veil (the inner sanctuary, heaven)," the anchor reaching up into heaven, fastened to Christ behind the veil (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the anchoring hope. The verse marks the figure: an anchor. The verse displays the firmness: sure and stedfast. The verse displays the anchorage: within the veil. The verse displays the security: in heaven. The hope displays the soul anchored to Christ in the sanctuary. The verse displays the anchor: of the soul. The verse displays the sureness: firm. The verse displays the veil: entered. The hope as an anchor entering within the veil displays the security of the believer's hope: it is the anchor of the soul, sure and steadfast, holding firm amid every storm — and reaching not into the shifting seabed of the world but up into the inner sanctuary, fastened to Christ behind the veil in heaven. The verse displays the soul's anchor in heaven, where Christ the forerunner has entered (v.20).
20:
"Whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus, made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec." The witnesses note Jesus the forerunner entered within the veil as high priest forever. On "whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus," the grammatical voices note the new title. Barnes and Robertson note the striking word: "whither (within the veil) the forerunner (prodromos — one who runs ahead, a pioneer who goes before to prepare the way for others) is for us entered, even Jesus" — Jesus has entered heaven as our forerunner; unlike the high priest who entered the earthly Holy of Holies alone, Jesus enters as a pioneer, that his people may follow.

On "forerunner," the eastern Cyril notes the distinctive: under the law, none might follow the high priest into the Holy of Holies; but Jesus enters as "forerunner" — going before to prepare a place, that all his people may come after him into the sanctuary; the title is unique to him here.

On "made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec," the grammatical-historical note the theme resumed: Jesus is "made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec (Psalm 110:4)" — the great subject now to be unfolded (ch. 7); his priesthood is eternal and after the Melchizedek order, not the Levitical. The verse displays Jesus the forerunner entered within the veil as high priest forever.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new title — "the forerunner (pioneer who goes ahead) is for us entered, even Jesus," Jesus entering heaven as our forerunner, that his people may follow (Barnes, Robertson); under the law none followed the high priest, but Jesus enters as forerunner to prepare a place (Cyril); "made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec" (Psalm 110:4), the eternal Melchizedek priesthood now to be unfolded (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the forerunner-priest. The verse marks the title: forerunner. The verse displays the entrance: within the veil. The verse displays the priesthood: for ever. The verse displays the order: Melchisedec. The verse displays Jesus entering heaven as priest and pioneer. The verse displays the forerunner: Jesus. The verse displays the entering: for us. The verse displays the priest: eternal. Jesus the forerunner entered within the veil as high priest forever displays the anchorage of the believer's hope: Jesus has entered heaven not alone but as our forerunner — a pioneer who goes before that his people may follow — made a high priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek. The verse displays the eternal high priest, whose Melchizedek order is now expounded (ch.7).
